beats.text: In this module, you built rectangles on the grid and found areas that are multiples of a side length. You filled in sentences like '20 is a multiple of 4 because 4 × 5 = 20.' You built at least two different rectangles — both times, the area was a multiple of the same side length. Let's look at what that tells us.

params.description: First completed sentence appears: 12 is a multiple of 4 because 4 × 3 = 12
Second completed sentence appears: 16 is a multiple of 4 because 4 × 4 = 16
Third completed sentence appears: 20 is a multiple of 4 because 4 × 5 = 20

beats.text: Look at all three sentences. What do you notice about the first blank in each one — the area number?
What do you notice about the area numbers?

validator.description: Student selected 'They all go up by 4', correct observation of the multiple pattern

beats.text: You noticed that every time you added one more row of 4 squares, the area went up by 4. That's what multiples do — they go up by the same amount each time.

validator.description: Student selected 'They are all even', observes surface property but misses the fixed increment pattern

beats.text: You noticed that every time you added one more row of 4 squares, the area went up by 4. That's what multiples do — they go up by the same amount each time.

validator.description: Student selected 'They are all the same', misreads the data

beats.text: You noticed that every time you added one more row of 4 squares, the area went up by 4. That's what multiples do — they go up by the same amount each time.

validator.description: Student selected 'I'm not sure', needs pattern support

beats.text: You noticed that every time you added one more row of 4 squares, the area went up by 4. That's what multiples do — they go up by the same amount each time.

elements.description: Completed sentence: 12 is a multiple of 4 because 4 × 3 = 12
Completed sentence: 16 is a multiple of 4 because 4 × 4 = 16
Completed sentence: 20 is a multiple of 4 because 4 × 5 = 20

params.description: A bookshelf illustration appears showing rows of books. Each row holds 7 books. Text reads: 'A bookshelf has rows of books. Each row holds 7 books. There are some rows on the shelf. Which of these could be the total number of books on the shelf?'

beats.text: Here's a bookshelf with rows of books. Each row holds 7 books. There are some rows on the shelf.
Which of these could be the total number of books on the shelf?

validator.description: Student selected 28 books, which is 4 × 7, a valid multiple of 7

beats.text: 28 is a multiple of 7 because 7 × 4 = 28. That could be on the shelf. Multiples of 7 show up everywhere rows of 7 things appear.

validator.description: Student selected 35 books, which is 5 × 7, a valid multiple of 7

beats.text: 35 is a multiple of 7 because 7 × 5 = 35. That could be on the shelf. Multiples of 7 show up everywhere rows of 7 things appear.

elements.description: Bookshelf illustration with rows of 7 books. Multiple choice options displayed: 23 books, 28 books, 31 books, 35 books.

beats.text: You built rectangles two ways today. First placing tiles freely, then using the handle to extend full rows.
Which way helped you see the rows more clearly?
options: Dragging the handle to extend a full row

validator.description: Student selected placing tiles one by one

beats.text: When you placed tiles, you had to decide where each one went. That takes focus. Good to know how you think.

condition.selected: Dragging the handle to extend a full row

beats.text: Dragging the handle adds a whole row at once. That makes the row structure easier to see.

validator.description: Student selected both ways helped equally

beats.text: Different moments, different tools. That kind of flexibility helps.
Still figuring it out is fine. You'll notice over time.
You built rectangles where one side stayed the same, and you found different areas — all multiples of that side length.
You can write: ___ is a multiple of ___ because ___ × ___ = ___.
In the next module, you're going to look at one of those rectangles one more time — and write the same equation in a second way.
You'll be ready because you already know what the equation means.
